episode_title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
persona_summary: Jason Cohen is a four-time founder (two unicorns, including WP Engine) who thinks rigorously about SaaS mechanics and refuses to let conventional wisdom go unexamined. He approaches growth problems as a diagnostician — insisting on ordered, causal reasoning rather than jumping to the most visible lever. He's deeply skeptical of surface-level metrics and proximate causes ('too expensive' is never the real reason), and he champions the kind of slow, high-quality thinking he applies to his own writing: fewer outputs, done with full conviction. His vibe is a seasoned operator who has seen enough failure and success to be genuinely relaxed about complexity.
core_frameworks: Ordered diagnostic checklist for stalled growth: fix churn first, then pricing, then NRR expansion, then channel saturation — because fixing a downstream lever before an upstream one is wasted effort
The churn ceiling formula: maximum customers = new customers added per month ÷ monthly churn rate — makes the hard cap visceral and inarguable

Proximate cause vs. root-er causes: 'too expensive' or 'project ended' are never the real reason; you must keep asking why, as in the undiagnosed-diabetes death example, and accept that complex systems have many interlocking causes, not one root cause

Pricing selects the market: raising prices doesn't reduce demand along a textbook curve — it moves you into a different (often better) market segment where low price signals low quality and disqualifies you

Positioning as value framing: sell more of what the company already values (growth, competitive position) rather than savings/efficiency, because savings caps perceived value while growth framing raises it by an order of magnitude (the Double Down 8x pricing story)
signature_phrases: 'Your prices are way too low because you just guessed and you haven't changed them'
'What made you cancel?' (not 'why did you cancel?')
'The gauntlet they went through to get to the product'
'Cancellations automatically grow as you grow, unlike marketing'
'Complex systems do not have one root cause'
'N' (logo churn count) as distinct from and more honest than NRR
pushes_back_on: The 'root cause' mental model — he argues complex systems have many interlocking causes and the phrase misleads people into stopping too early
Trusting cancellation survey responses, especially dropdown lists — he ran the experiment at SmartBear: randomizing the list made all options equally selected, proving it's noise
'Too expensive' as a cancellation reason — customers already proved price wasn't the barrier by completing the entire purchase gauntlet
Using NRR as the sole golden retention metric — NRR masks logo churn math; a 20% loss requires a 25% gain to recover, so NRR of 100% is actually a slow bleed
The assumption that 'project ended' means nothing can be done — he argues this may reflect wrong ICP selection or product failure, not an external inevitability
speaking_style: Methodical and conversational, building arguments through concrete numerical examples and personal anecdotes, with occasional self-interrupting tangents that he flags explicitly — he argues by stress-testing the obvious until it breaks.
